serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of seaweed soup with pork is approximately 400 g, as commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres and coffee shops.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'seaweed soup with pork' exists in the HPB Singapore food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated by component-level adaptation using HPB data for pork (lean, boiled), dried laver seaweed, and clear broth, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for seaweed (laver, raw; USDA #168457) and pork (fresh, loin, lean, cooked, braised; USDA #168351). The input record values (energy 45 kcal, protein 3.75 g, fat 2.5 g, carbohydrate 2.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with a dilute soup containing approximately 5–8% pork and 1–2% dried seaweed by weight and are retained as the best available estimate. All values are flagged as medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seaweed soup with pork. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with clear meat-based soups and broth-based dishes with minimal starch, consistent with values reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) for low-carbohydrate soups and broths (GI range 25–40). Carbohydrate per 100 g is 2.0 g; GL per 400 g serving = (35 × 8.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.8. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and diluted broth to lower the sodium load per serving; seaweed is naturally high in sodium and should be consumed in moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is very low at 2.0 g per 100 g with negligible sugar (0.25 g per 100 g), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 2.8 per 400 g bowl.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seaweed soup with pork is a suitable low-glycaemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, commercial preparations may contain added sugars in the broth or soy sauce, so patients should verify ingredients or request homemade preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g and cholesterol is moderate at 11.25 mg per 100 g; however, sodium at 300 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, this soup is acceptable in small portions with reduced soy sauce; the pork content contributes saturated fat and cholesterol, so substituting with tofu or egg white would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with tofu, egg, or fish to reduce arachidonic acid intake while retaining the anti-inflammatory minerals and iodine provided by seaweed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seaweed Soup with Pork (紫菜汤, Zi Cai Tang) is a light, clear Chinese-style soup commonly served in Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and home kitchens. The dish is prepared by simmering dried laver seaweed (紫菜, zǐ cài) in a clear pork or chicken broth, with thin slices or minced pork added for protein and umami depth. The soup is seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, and salt, producing a savoury, mildly briny flavour with a silky, tender texture from the rehydrated seaweed. Eggs or tofu are sometimes added as protein alternatives or supplements. The broth is clear and light golden in colour, with dark green-black seaweed ribbons and pale pork pieces. Nutritionally, this is a low-calorie, low-carbohydrate soup providing approximately 45 kcal per 100 g, with 3.75 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and only 2.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 180 kcal. Sodium content is moderate to high at 300 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per bowl), which is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The glycaemic load per serving is very low at approximately 2.8, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Seaweed provides iodine, calcium, and dietary fibre. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content, and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute pork with plant-based protein to reduce arachidonic acid intake.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for seaweed soup with pork exists in the HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from component-level data for dried laver seaweed and lean boiled pork from approved sources (HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy). The input record values are internally consistent and plausible for a dilute broth-based soup. GI is an analogous estimate from approved literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific seaweed soup with pork preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was reviewed but does not provide a specific entry for this dish. Cultural tags in the input record incorrectly referenced 'Bak Kut Teh' and 'Malaysian' cuisine; this dish is a standard Singaporean Chinese seaweed soup (紫菜汤) and has been reclassified accordingly. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Trans fat is reported at 0.03 g per 100 g, consistent with trace levels from pork fat; this is a confirmed trace value from the component-level source adaptation. Calcium is reported at 15.0 mg per 100 g and iron at 0.38 mg per 100 g, consistent with seaweed and pork contributions per USDA SR Legacy data.
